cards.text:
A constexpr if statement syntax: {{c1::if constexpr (condition)}}
{
    // statement
}

cards.front: What is the key difference between if and if constexpr?
cards.back: if constexpr is evaluated at compile-time and the condition must be a constant expression. Regular if is evaluated at runtime.

cards.front: What happens to the non-taken branch of an if constexpr statement?
cards.back: The non-taken branch is discarded at compile-time and does not appear in the compiled program. It's as if that code never existed.

cards.text: The condition in an if constexpr statement must be a {{c1::constant expression}} that can be evaluated at {{c2::compile-time}}.

cards.question: Will this compile?
cards.explanation: The condition must be a constant expression. Even though x is initialized to 5, it's not a const or constexpr variable, so x > 3 is not a constant expression.

cards.question: Will this compile?
cards.explanation: x is constexpr, so x > 3 is a constant expression that can be evaluated at compile-time. The compiler will evaluate it to true and discard the else branch if present.

cards.front: What is the primary use case for if constexpr in modern C++?
cards.back: Template metaprogramming - to conditionally compile different code paths based on template parameters or type traits, evaluated at compile-time.

cards.concept: When to use if constexpr vs regular if
cards.explanation:
Use if constexpr when:
  • The condition can be evaluated at compile-time
  • You want to eliminate dead code from the binary
  • Working with templates and type traits
  • Optimizing performance-critical code
Use regular if when:
  • The condition depends on runtime values
  • You need runtime flexibility
  • The condition involves user input or external data

cards.text: Code that is removed during compilation and doesn't appear in the final executable is called {{c1::dead code}} or {{c2::unreachable code}}.

cards.front: In which C++ standard was if constexpr introduced?
What are the two main benefits of using if constexpr?
cards.back:
1) Smaller binary size (dead code is eliminated)
2) Faster execution (no runtime conditional evaluation needed)
